title: Getting Around Marseille — Transport & Travel Guide
content:
To get around Marseille, you can use a variety of transportation options including metro, bus, train, and taxi, as well as ride-hailing apps and car rentals, making it relatively easy to navigate the city and its surroundings.

[FACTBOX]
Main International Airport(s): Marseille Provence Airport (MRS)
Domestic Air Network: extensive
Rail Network: extensive
Drives On: right
Road Quality: good
Ride-Hailing Apps: Uber, Bolt, local alternatives
[/FACTBOX]

[SECTION] Getting There [/SECTION]
Marseille is easily accessible by air, with Marseille Provence Airport (MRS) being the main international airport serving the city. The airport is located about 27 kilometers northwest of Marseille's city center and offers direct flights to many European cities, as well as connections to destinations in North Africa, the Middle East, and other parts of the world. You can also reach Marseille by train, with the city's Saint-Charles station being a major hub for high-speed trains to Paris, Lyon, and other French cities, as well as international trains to Italy, Spain, and other European countries.

In addition to air and train travel, Marseille can also be reached by car or bus, with several major highways and roads connecting the city to other parts of France and Europe. The city is also a major port, with ferry services connecting Marseille to destinations in the Mediterranean, such as Corsica, Sardinia, and North Africa. If you're traveling from within Europe, you can also consider taking a bus or coach, with companies like FlixBus and Eurolines offering affordable and convenient options.

[SECTION] Domestic Flights [/SECTION]
France has an extensive domestic air network, with several airlines operating flights within the country. If you're looking to travel to other parts of France from Marseille, you can consider flying with airlines like Air France, Ryanair, or EasyJet. Domestic flights can be booked online or through a travel agent, and prices vary depending on the route, time of year, and how far in advance you book. On average, a domestic flight within France can cost anywhere from $50 to $200 or more, depending on the route and airline.

[SECTION] Trains & Rail [/SECTION]
France has an extensive rail network, with high-speed trains like the TGV (Train à Grande Vitesse) connecting major cities like Paris, Lyon, and Marseille. The TGV is a convenient and relatively affordable way to travel, with prices starting from around $60 for a one-way ticket. You can book train tickets online or at the station, and it's a good idea to book in advance to secure the best prices. Marseille's Saint-Charles station is a major hub for trains, with frequent departures to destinations throughout France and Europe.

[SECTION] Buses & Coaches [/SECTION]
If you're looking for a more affordable option, you can consider taking a bus or coach. Several companies, including FlixBus and Eurolines, operate bus services within France and to other European destinations. Bus travel can be a good option for shorter journeys, and prices are often significantly cheaper than trains or flights. However, journey times can be longer, and the quality of the buses and services can vary.

[SECTION] City Transport [/SECTION]
| Mode | Available In | Cost (USD) | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Metro/Subway | Marseille | $1.80 | Two metro lines, 32 stations |
| City Bus | Marseille | $1.80 | Extensive network of buses, including night buses |
| Taxi | Everywhere | $2.50/km | Available at taxi ranks or by phone |
| Ride-Hailing | Marseille | $5-$10 | Uber, Bolt, and local alternatives available |
| Bike Rental | Marseille | $10-$20/day | Several bike rental companies, including VeloMarseille |

[SECTION] Driving [/SECTION]
- To drive in France, you'll need a valid driver's license from your home country, as well as car insurance that covers you for driving in France.
- The road quality in Marseille and surrounding areas is generally good, with well-maintained highways and roads.
- Fuel costs can be high in France, with gasoline and diesel prices among the highest in Europe.
- If you're planning to rent a car, it's a good idea to book in advance to secure the best prices, and be sure to check the rental car's insurance policy and any additional fees.
- Be aware of the city's traffic rules and regulations, including speed limits, parking restrictions, and pedestrian zones.
- If you're driving in Marseille, be prepared for narrow streets and heavy traffic in the city center, and consider using a GPS or map to navigate the city's one-way streets and pedestrian zones.

[SECTION] Transport Tips [/SECTION]
- Be aware of pickpocketing and scams in crowded areas and tourist hotspots, and keep a close eye on your belongings, especially in busy train stations and airports.
- Consider purchasing a **Marseille City Pass**, which includes public transportation and discounts on attractions and activities.
- Download the **Citymapper** or **Google Maps** app to help navigate the city's public transportation system and plan your routes.
- Be prepared for strikes or disruptions to public transportation, and check for updates before traveling.
- Consider using a **rechargeable ticket** or **travel card** for public transportation, which can be more convenient and cost-effective than buying individual tickets.
- Be mindful of the city's **traffic rules** and regulations, including speed limits, parking restrictions, and pedestrian zones.
- If you're taking a taxi, be sure to check the meter and agree on the fare before you start your journey, and consider using a reputable taxi company or ride-hailing app.
- Consider purchasing **travel insurance** that covers you for unexpected medical or travel-related expenses, and be sure to check the policy's coverage and exclusions before you travel.
